labels.name: Endpoint
labels.desc: A URL path the API exposes

labels.name: Service layer
labels.desc: Business logic between controller and data

labels.name: Repository
labels.desc: Reading and writing to the database

labels.name: Relationship
labels.desc: A data connection between two entities — one-to-many, many-to-many

labels.name: Validation
labels.desc: Enforcing rules on incoming data before it is accepted or saved

labels.name: Error handling
labels.desc: Catching problems and returning meaningful responses instead of stack traces

prompts.title: Order Items Endpoint
prompts.statement: A client needs to see all the line items in a specific order — product name and quantity for each. The order is identified by its ID in the URL.

expectedTags.phrase: identified by its ID in the URL
expectedTags.explanation: The order ID embedded in the URL path is how the client communicates which order they want — that makes it both the endpoint address and the incoming request data.

expectedTags.phrase: see all the line items in a specific order
expectedTags.explanation: Fetching line items for an order means traversing a one-to-many relationship between orders and items; the service layer handles this lookup and coordination.

expectedTags.phrase: product name and quantity for each
expectedTags.explanation: This is the data retrieved from the database via the repository and then packaged into the response sent back to the client.

prompts.title: Add to Wishlist
prompts.statement: Users can save products to their wishlist. An endpoint should accept a user ID and a product ID and create the association. If either doesn't exist, the client should receive a clear error.

expectedTags.phrase: accept a user ID and a product ID
expectedTags.explanation: The two IDs passed to the endpoint are the request data the client provides to identify what should be linked.

expectedTags.phrase: create the association
expectedTags.explanation: Linking a user to a product is a many-to-many relationship; persisting that link is a write operation handled by the repository.

expectedTags.phrase: If either doesn't exist, the client should receive a clear error
expectedTags.explanation: Checking whether both IDs are valid before proceeding is business logic that lives in the service layer, and returning a meaningful message when one is missing is error handling.

prompts.title: Customer Profile
prompts.statement: A customer profile endpoint should return a complete picture: the customer's info, their current address, their recent orders, and their total spend across all orders.

expectedTags.phrase: customer profile endpoint
expectedTags.explanation: The customer profile URL is the address the client calls, and the customer ID supplied with that call is the request data used to identify whose profile to load.

expectedTags.phrase: their current address, their recent orders, and their total spend
expectedTags.explanation: Address and orders are related entities connected to the customer; retrieving them requires traversing those relationships and reading from the database via the repository.

expectedTags.phrase: return a complete picture
expectedTags.explanation: Assembling multiple pieces of related data into one cohesive profile is the job of the service layer, and the finished object is what gets sent back as the response.

prompts.title: Submit Product Review
prompts.statement: Customers can leave reviews on products. The review must include a rating between 1 and 5 and a non-empty comment. Both rules should be enforced before the review is saved.

expectedTags.phrase: leave reviews on products
expectedTags.explanation: Submitting a review is the client request arriving at the endpoint, and a review belonging to a product is a many-to-one relationship between those two entities.

expectedTags.phrase: a rating between 1 and 5 and a non-empty comment
expectedTags.explanation: These are explicit data rules that must be checked on the incoming review before it is accepted — that is exactly what validation enforces.

expectedTags.phrase: before the review is saved
expectedTags.explanation: The service layer coordinates the validation check and business logic, and the repository is responsible for actually persisting the review to the database.

prompts.title: Product Search Endpoint
prompts.statement: A product search API accepts up to three optional filters: a name keyword, a minimum price, and a maximum price. If none are provided, all products should be returned.

expectedTags.phrase: product search API
expectedTags.explanation: The search URL is the endpoint the client calls to initiate a filtered product lookup.

expectedTags.phrase: up to three optional filters: a name keyword, a minimum price, and a maximum price
expectedTags.explanation: These filter values are query parameters the client sends along with the request to narrow down the results.

expectedTags.phrase: If none are provided
expectedTags.explanation: Deciding which filters to apply — or skipping them entirely when none are given — is conditional business logic that belongs in the service layer.

expectedTags.phrase: all products should be returned
expectedTags.explanation: Retrieving the full product list when no filters are set is a database read via the repository, and the resulting list is sent back as the response.

prompts.title: Account Transfer
prompts.statement: A banking API endpoint accepts a transfer request — a source account, a destination account, and an amount. If either account isn't found, or if there aren't enough funds, the client should receive a descriptive error.

expectedTags.phrase: accepts a transfer request — a source account, a destination account, and an amount
expectedTags.explanation: The URL that handles transfers is the endpoint, and the source account, destination account, and amount are the data the client sends in the request body.

expectedTags.phrase: If either account isn't found
expectedTags.explanation: Looking up both accounts by ID is a repository read; the service layer is responsible for coordinating those lookups and deciding how to proceed.

expectedTags.phrase: if there aren't enough funds
expectedTags.explanation: Checking the account balance before allowing a transfer is a business rule enforced like a validation, and rejecting the operation with a clear message is error handling.

expectedTags.phrase: the client should receive a descriptive error
expectedTags.explanation: Whether the problem is a missing account or insufficient funds, the API must send back a meaningful message — that message is the response the client receives.

prompts.title: Global Validation Error Handler
prompts.statement: When a client submits invalid data, the API should respond with a structured list of all the field-level problems — not just a generic error message or a stack trace.

expectedTags.phrase: client submits invalid data
expectedTags.explanation: The client's submission is the incoming request, and the fact that the data breaks the defined rules is a validation failure.

expectedTags.phrase: structured list of all the field-level problems
expectedTags.explanation: Collecting each validation failure into a structured format is error handling, and sending that structure back to the client is the response.

expectedTags.phrase: not just a generic error message or a stack trace
expectedTags.explanation: Good error handling means returning meaningful, actionable feedback rather than exposing internal server details or an unhelpful generic message.

prompts.title: Monthly Sales Summary
prompts.statement: A reporting endpoint returns a sales summary grouped by month for a given year. The data is drawn from the relationship between orders and their line items, and the year is passed in as a query parameter.

expectedTags.phrase: reporting endpoint
expectedTags.explanation: The sales summary URL is the endpoint clients call to request aggregated sales data.

expectedTags.phrase: year is passed in as a query parameter
expectedTags.explanation: The year value is data the client sends with the request to scope the report to a specific time period.

expectedTags.phrase: relationship between orders and their line items
expectedTags.explanation: Orders and line items are connected by a one-to-many relationship; querying across that association to aggregate data is the repository's responsibility.

expectedTags.phrase: sales summary grouped by month
expectedTags.explanation: Grouping and aggregating sales data by month is business logic coordinated by the service layer, and the resulting summary is what gets sent back as the response.
